steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team's claim rests on a ten-year-old survey about willingness to walk, plus a general finding that sidewalks and crosswalks increase that willingness. The question asks which new finding best supports placing the bus stop farther away.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The number of sidewalks and crosswalks in the neighborhood that the streetcar stop will service has increased substantially in the last ten years.: More paved infrastructure means residents are now more willing to walk farther than the old survey showed, directly supporting the stop placement. ✓
  • Choice B — Residents of Detroit are much less likely to use public transit on rainy days than on clear days.: Weather patterns don't speak to pedestrian infrastructure or willingness to walk to a specific stop. ✗
  • Choice C — There has been a sharp increase in the last ten years in cyclists who use the roads in the neighborhood that the streetcar stop will service.: About cyclists, not pedestrians; irrelevant to the bus stop placement claim. ✗
  • Choice D — Current users of the Woodward Avenue Streetcar are satisfied with the number of stops along the line.: Satisfaction with existing service doesn't address whether residents will walk farther to a new stop location. ✗
That matches Choice A — The number of sidewalks and crosswalks in the neighborhood that the streetcar stop will service has increased substantially in the last ten years..
